92.10: Cf. Ep. lxxi. 16 non intenditur virtus. The Stoic idea of tension may be combined here with the raising of a note to a higher pitch.
92.11: Frag. 138 Usener. Cf. Sen. Ep. lxvi. 47.
92.12: Vergil, Aeneid, v. 363. Vergil MSS. read pectore.
92.13: i.e., to participation in the divine existence.
92.15: Cf. Juvenal, x. 65 Seianus ducitur unco spectandus. The bodies of criminals were dragged by the hook through the city to the Scalae Gemoniae, down which they were flung.
92.17: The figure is taken from the Roman dress,—one who was “girt high” (alte cinctus), ready for vigorous walking, being contrasted with the loosely-girdled person (discinctus), indolent or effeminate. On the character of Maecenas see Epp. cxiv. 4 ff., xix. 9, cxx. 19.

93.1: A philosopher of Naples, mentioned as giving lectures there: cf. Ep. lxxvi. 4.
93.3: For a complete definition of the Supreme Good cf. Ep. lxxi. 4 ff.
93.5: For the same phrase see Ep. lxvi. 30 and footnote.
93.6: Cf. Ep. lx. 4 mortem suam antecesserunt.
93.8: As in the original comitia centuriata, men between the ages of seventeen and forty-six.
93.11: See, however, Seneca, N. Q. vii. 2. 3 sciamus utrum mundus terra stante circumeat an mundo stante terra vertatur.  For doubts and discoveries cf. Arnold, Roman Stoicism, pp. 178 f.

94.1: For technical terms in Epp. xciv. and xcv. see Appendix.
94.2: See Cicero, De Off. i. 3. 7 ff. for a full discussion of principles and duties.  As one would expect, the Romans were more interested in practical precepts than were the Greeks.
94.4: In other words, that it is one of the “external” things, media, indifferentia.
94.5: Compare, among similar passages, Ep. xxiv. 14 levis es, si ferre possum, brevis es, si ferre non possum.
94.6: For the same figure, in the same connexion, see Ep. lxviii. 8 in pectore ipso collectio et vomica est.
94.7: By means of hellebore, Lat. veratrum, the favourite cathartic of the ancients.
94.8: This is in harmony with the idea of Socrates; sin is a lack of knowledge regarding what is true and what is false.
94.10: monitio includes consolatio, dissuasio, obiurgatio, laudatio, and hortatio.  Cf. § 39 of this letter
94.11: Quoted also by Quintilian, vi. 1. 13.  Between the years 58 and 54 B.C. Calvus, a friend of the poet Catullus, in three famous speeches prosecuted Vatinius, one of the creatures of Caesar who had illegally obtained office.
94.14: A verse made up from Vergil, Aen. x. 284, and an unknown author.
94.16: A further answer to the objection in § 17 above, where all madness is held curable by physical treatment.
94.17: See, for example, the Fifth Book, which opens with the preliminary remarks of the Athenian Stranger (pp. 726-34 St.).
94.18: A frequent thought in Seneca, cf. Ep. xxv. 6, lii. 8, etc.
94.19: Presumably Phaedo the friend of Plato and pupil of Socrates, author of dialogues resembling those of Plato.
94.20: Com. incert., Frag. 81 Ribbeck, and Pub. Syrus, Frag. 2 Ribbeck.
94.23: Cf. Ep. xciv. 12 exactum indicium de fugiendis petendisque.
94.24: The last stage of knowledge—complete assent—according to the Stoic view, which went beyond the mere sensation-theory of Epicurus.
94.25: In this whole discussion Seneca is a much sounder Stoic than Aristo and the opposition.  The next letter (Ep. xcv.) develops still further the preceptive function of philosophy—through προκοπή (progress) to μεταβολή (conversion).
94.26: This theme is carefully elaborated in Ep. vii., “On Crowds”: “There is no person who does not make some vice attractive to us, or stamp it upon us, or taint us unconsciously therewith” (§ 2).
94.27: These are fully discussed in Seneca’s Naturales Quaestiones, a work almost contemporary with the Letters.
94.28: Both literally and figuratively,—the sheen of the metal and the glitter of the false idea.
94.29: i.e., the bundle of rods and axes, carried by the attendants of a Roman magistrate.
94.30: A name usually applied to the eastern end of the Mediterranean.
94.31: Especially Thebes in 335 B.C., which he sacked.  Athens and Sparta were treated with more consideration.
94.32: i.e., the Hyrcanians, and other tribes attacked during and after 330 B.C.
94.33: Heracles in his various forms hails all the way from Tyre to the Atlantic Ocean; Dionysus from India through Lydia, Thrace, and the Eastern Mediterranean to Greece.
94.36: Beginning with the passage of the Manilian Law of 66 B.C.
94.38: 102 and 101 B.C. at Aquae Sextiae and Vercellae; the Jugurthine war lasted from 109 to 106 B.C.

95.1: Literally, to pay money on the spot or perform a task without delay.
95.2: i.e., the department of “advice by precepts,” discussed in the preceding letter from another angle.  The Greek term is nearest to the Latin sub-division hortatio.
95.3: i.e., the pertness of a home-bred slave (verna).
95.4: The argument here is similar to Ep. lxxxviii. 20 hae . . . artes ad instrumenta vitae plurimum conferunt, tamen ad virtutem non pertinent.
95.6: Hippocrates belonged to the “Clinical” School; Asclepiades and his pupil Themison to the “Methodical.” See Index of Proper Names.
95.9: Whether elementa and membra mean “letters and clauses” or “matter and forms of matter” is difficult to say.
95.10: i.e., before the advent of any theoretical philosophy.
95.11: verminatio, defined by Festus as cum corpus quodam minuto motu quasi a vermibus scindatur.
95.12: For this sort of Golden Age reminiscence see Ep. xc. 5 ff. and note.
95.14: Mushrooms, as in the case of the Emperor Claudius, were a frequent aid to secret murder.
95.15: The finest variety of garum was made from Spanish mackerel-roe.
95.17: Cf. Ep. xxxvii. 1 uri, vinciri, ferroque necari and note.
95.18: i.e., not reinforced by general dogmas.
95.19: The nota was the mark of disgrace which the censor registered when he struck a man’s name off the list of senators or knights.
95.20: The genius was properly a man’s alter ego or “better self”: every man had his genius. For the colloquial use compare the “indulge genio” of the Roman poets.
95.22: A frequent vice under the Empire, nicknamed captatio.
95.23: Περὶ καθήκοντος,—a subject handled by Panaetius, and by Cicero (De Officiis).
95.24: i.e., the significant features of athletics and adornment for men and women respectively.
95.27: i.e., progressing from a φαντασία in general to a φαντασία καταληπτική.
95.28: Seneca characteristically ignores the unpleasant half of the proverb: φιλεῖν ὡς μισήσων καὶ μισεῖν ὡς φιλήσων.
95.29: e.g., in the mysteries of Eleusis, etc.
95.30: For these terms see Spengel, Rhet. Graec., passim. Quintilian i. 9. 3 says ethologia personis continetur; and Cicero, De Orat. iii. 205, in a list of figures with which the orator should be familiar, includes characterismos, or descriptio.
95.31: For the same figure, similarly applied, see Ep. lxxx. 9 and note.
95.33: For example, Cato had from the first opposed any assumption of illegal power,—objecting to the consulship of Pompey and Crassus in 55 B.C., and to the conduct of Caesar throughout. His disapproval of both simultaneously is hinted in Plutarch’s Cato the Younger, liv. 4.
95.34: The Latin term can hardly be reproduced, though “he did not regale but regulate” comes near it. Tubero’s act was that of a true censor morum.

96.1: Cf. the words ducunt volentem fata, nolentem trahunt of Ep. cvii. 11.

97.1: For the best account of this scandal see Plutarch, Caesar, ix. f.
97.2: From stilla “a drop.” The phrase is equivalent to our proverbial “last straw.”
97.4: A plebeian festival, held April 28, in honour of Flora, an Italian divinity connected with Ceres and Venus. For the story of Cato (55 B.C.) see Valer. Max. ii. 10. 8.

98.1: Compare the ἔχω ἀλλ᾽ οὐκ ἔχομαι of Aristippus, and the (equally Epicurean) mihi res, non me rebus subiungere of Horace, Epp. i. 1. 19.
98.3: i.e., a sort of platform for mountebanks or acrobats,—figuratively applied to life’s Vanity Fair.
98.5: i.e., when he declined the bribe of Pyrrhus, 280 B.C.
98.6: Cf. Ep. xcv. 72 f. omnibus saeculis Tuberonis fictilio durabunt.
98.7: Cf. Ep. lix. 7 and note b (vol. i.).
98.8: The testimony of an ancient grammarian, and the change of subject in the text, may, as Hense states, indicate that a considerable passage is lost and that another letter begins here. Cf. the senex egregius of § 15.

99.1: Possibly Iunius Marullus, consul designatus in A.D. 62 (Tac. Ann. xiv. 48).
99.2: As Lipsius pointed out, the remainder of Seneca’s letter consists of the quoted letter to Marullus.
99.3: The Roman view differs from the modern view, just as this Letter is rather more severe than Ep. lxiii. (on the death of Lucilius’s friend Flaccus).
99.4: Almost identical language with the closing words of Ep. lxiii.: quem putamus perisse, praemissus est.
99.5: For a similar argument see Ep. xii. 6 f.
99.6: i.e., who have had to turn gladiators.
99.7: i.e., a shroud for the funeral couch, lectus vitalis.
99.8: This passage, which Buecheler corrected in several places, is omitted in the English, because Seneca has already translated it literally. M. was addressing his sister.
99.12: i.e., grief should not be replaced by pleasure; otherwise grief will cease to exist.

100.1: i.e., his style is like a river rather than a torrent.
100.2: Concisura: from concido, to “cut into sections,” “distribute” (of water-pipes).
100.3: Cf. Ep. xviii. 7, and Martial iii. 48:Pauperis extruxit cellam, sed vendidit Oluspraedia; nunc cellam pauperis Olus habet.Rich men sometimes fitted up in their palaces an imitation “poor man’s cabin” by way of contrast to their other rooms or as a gesture towards simple living; Seneca uses the phrase figuratively for certain devices in composition.
100.4: Quintilian x. 1. 113 says: multa in Asinio Pollione inventio, summa diligentia, adeo ut quibusdam etiam nimia videatur; et consilii et animi satis; a nitore et iucunditate Ciceronis ita longe abest, ut videri possit saeculo prior.
100.5: The wording here resembles strikingly that of the Elder Seneca, Controv. ii. pr. 2 deerat illi (sc. Fabiano) oratorium robur et ille pugnatorius mucro.

101.2: Perhaps a hint to Lucilius, who was at this time procurator in Sicily.
101.4: Horace, his intimate friend, wrote Od. ii. 17 to cheer the despondent Maecenas; and Pliny (N. H. vii. 54) mentions his fevers and his insomnia—perpetua febris. . . . Eidem triennio supremo nullo horae momento contigit somnus.
101.6: Infelix lignum (or arbor) is the cross.

102.1: Seneca, worn out by his political experiences, was at this time not less than sixty-seven years of age.
102.2: Seneca is perhaps popularizing the Stoic combinations,—παράθεσις (juxtaposition), μῖξις (mixture) or κρᾶσις (fusion), and σύγχυσις (chemical mixture). Cf. E. V. Arnold, Roman Stoicism, p. 169.
102.3: i.e., the arguments of the Stoics.
102.4: i.e., of the unus vir bonus, as contrasted with the many.
102.6: Naevius, quoted by Cicero, Tusc. Disp. iv. 31 (of Hector):laetus sumlaudari me abs te, pater, laudato viro.
102.7: A commonplace sentiment, found e.g., in Cicero, Tusc. Disp. i. 2. 4.
102.8: Cf. Ep. xl. 4 haec popularis (oratio) nihil habet veri.
102.9: A metaphor from the arena: decretoria were real decisive weapons with which death was faced, as opposed to lusoria, “sham” weapons. Cf. Sen. Ep. cxvii. 25.
102.10: The departure from life is compared to the release from the womb. There is also possibly a double meaning implied in the word venter.

103.1: Compare this with the Seventh letter (vol. i.).

104.1: Pompeia Paulina, the second wife of Seneca; cf. Tac. Ann. xv. 60. Though much younger than her husband, she was a model of devotion, and remained loyal to him through all the Neronian persecution.
104.2: Elder brother of Seneca, whose name before his adoption by Lucius Iunius Gallio was Annaeus Novatus. He was governor of Achaia from A.D. July 1, 51 to July 1, 52. See Acts xviii. 11 ff., and Duff, Three Dialogues of Seneca, p. xliii.
104.3: Cf. Ep. x. 1 “Mecum loquor.” “Cave, rogo, et diligenter adtende; cum homine malo loqueris.”
104.6: Although Seneca was deeply interested in such matters, as is proved by Ep. lxxix., the Naturales Quaestiones, and an early work on the geography of Egypt.
104.7: These men are patterns or interpreters of the virtues. The first-named three represent courage, justice, and self-restraint respectively. Socrates is the ideal wise man, Zeno, Chrysippus, and Posidonus are in turn the founder, the classifier, and the modernizer of Stoicism.
104.9: At first a sculptor, then an independent seeker after truth, whose wants were reduced to a minimum. Husband of the shrewish Xanthippe and father of the dull and worthless Lamprocles. Brave soldier at Potidaea, Delium, and Amphipolis.
104.11: See Plato’s Apology, 23 D. They had previously aimed at him a law forbidding the teaching of dialectic.
104.13: Triumvirs in 60 B.C. and rivals in acquiring unconstitutional power.
104.15: Perhaps a reference to his mission in Cyprus (58–56 B.C.), and his subsequent arraignment by Clodius.

106.1: Presumably (cf. Ep. cviii. § 1) into this collection of Epistles.
106.2: As Lucilius, in his letter, has come from far away.
106.3: This subject is discussed more fully in Ep. cxiii. For a clear account of the whole question of “body” see Arnold, Roman Stoicism, pp. 157 ff.
106.5: The Romans had a ludus latrunculorum, with features resembling both draughts and chess. The pieces (calculi) were perhaps of different values: the latrunculus may have been a sort of “rover,” cf. Martial, Epig. vii. 72.

107.2: Cleanthes, Frag. 527 von Arnim. In Epictetus (Ench. 53) these verses are assigned to Cleanthes (omitting the last line); while St. Augustine (Civ. Dei. v. 8) quotes them as Seneca’s: Annaei Senecae sunt, nisi fallor, hi versus. Wilamowitz and others follow the latter view.

108.1: Cf. Ep. cvi. 2 scis enim me moralem philosophiam velle conplecti, etc.
108.2: Seneca’s first and most convincing teacher of Stoicism, to whom this letter is a tribute. The ablest of contemporary philosophers, he was banished during the reign of Tiberius. See Indexes to Vols. I. and II.
108.4: Cf. the dangers of such lusoria (Ep. xlviii. 8) and a rebus studium transferendum est ad verba (Ep. xl. 14).
108.5: i.e., mendicant Galli, worshippers of Cybele, the Magna Mater.
108.11: An almost proverbial saying; cf. the recte olet ubi nil olet of Plautus (Most. 273), Cicero, and Martial.
108.12: Pythagorean philosopher of the Augustine age, and one of Seneca’s early teachers.
108.13: A.D. 19. Cf. Tacitus, Ann. ii. 85 actum de sacris Aegyptiis Iudaicisque pellendis.
108.14: In this passage Seneca differs (as also in Ep. lxxxviii. § 3) from the earlier Roman idea of grammaticus as poetarum interpres: he is thinking of one who deals with verbal expressions and the meaning of words. Cf. Sandys, Hist. Class. Schol. i. 8 ff.
108.18: Cicero, De re publica, ii. 18 Numae Pompili nepos ex filia rex a populo est Ancus Marcius constitutus . . . siquidem istius regis matrem habemus, ignoramus patrem.
108.19: Fl. in the Augustan Age. Provocatio is defined by Greenidge (Rom. Pub. Life. p. 64) as “a challenge by an accused to a magistrate to appear before another tribunal.”
108.20: A suffix, probably related to the intensive -pte
108.21: Literally, the chalk-marked, or lime-marked, goal-line.

109.1: i.e., in possession of a perfect, an encyclopaedic, wisdom.
109.2: In other words, Wisdom, Justice, Courage, and Self-Restraint, together with the other qualities of simplicity, kindness, etc., being “avatars” of Virtue herself, are interrelated.
109.3: e.g., certain of the Peripatetic school.
109.4: Sallust, Cat. xx. 4 idem velle atque idem nolle, ea demum firma amicitia est. Cf. the Greek “ἴσῳ ζυγῷ,” “yoked equally together.”

110.2: Metam. i. 595,—a Roman interpretation, along the lines of the Di Indigetes.
110.3: Every man had his Genius, and every woman her Juno. In the case of the Stoics, God dwelt in every soul.

112.1: Seneca was an extensive and prosperous vine-grower. Compare Ep. civ. 6 f. for his description of his hobby at the country-place near Nomentum. There are many figures which deal with the vine scattered through the Letters.

113.1: The fulfilment of the promise made in Ep. cvi. 3 (see note ad loc.).
113.2: The allusion is sarcastic. The phaecasium was a white shoe worn by Greek priests and Athenian gymnasiarchs,—sometimes aped by Romans.
113.3: i.e., animal from animus, anima (“breath of life”).
113.4: i.e., from those who hold that the man, the soul, and the functions of the soul, can be classed as separate entities; or even from those who believe that it is worth while to discuss the matter at all. See § 1 of this Letter.
113.5: Homer, Il. vi. 181 πρόσθε λέων, ὄπιθεν δὲ δράκων, μέσση δὲ χίμαιρα. This is a frequent illustration of the “whole and the parts” among ancient philosophers.
113.6: i.e., the form in which it is contained.
113.7: The soul is “body,” “world-stuff” (not “matter” in the modern sense). It is therefore, according to the Stoics, a living entity, a unit; and Virtue is a διάθεσις ψυχῆς,—a “permanent disposition of the soul.”
113.8: The usual progression was αἴσθησις (sensus), φαντασία (species, “external impression”), συγκατάθεσις (adsensus), and κατάληψις (comprehensio). See Ep. xcv. 62 note.
113.9: This problem is discussed from another angle in Ep. lviii. 16.
113.11: Cleanthes, Frag. 525 von Arnim; Chrysippus, Frag. 836 von Arnim. The former would seem to be more in accord with general Stoic views.
113.12: Caecilianum (the reading of the later MSS.) would refer to Statius Caecilius, the comic writer of the second century B.C. Caelianum (B and A) would indicate M. Caelius Rufus, the orator and contemporary of Cicero and Catullus.
113.14: See Ep. xciv. 63 f., and notes.
113.15: e.g., the execution of Parmenio in Media and the murder of Cleitus in Samarkand.

114.1: οῖος ὁ βίος, τοιοῦτος καὶ ὁ λόγος. The saying is referred to Socrates by Cicero (Tusc. v. 47).
114.2: i.e., that inborn quality which is compounded of character and intelligence.
114.3: Cf. Suetonius, Aug. 86, where the Emperor Maecenatem suum, cuius “myrobrechis,” ut ait, “cincinnos" (“unguent-dripping curls” (Rolfe)) usque quaque persequitur et imitando per iocum irridet. Augustus here refers especially to the style of Maecenas as a writer.
114.4: Terentia. For her charms see Horace, Od. ii. 12; for her faults see De prov. iii. 10, where Seneca calls her “petulant.”
114.6: Instead of properly girt up—a mark of slackness.
114.7: For a similar mark of slovenliness, in Pompey’s freedman Demetrius, see Plutarch, Pompey, xl. 4.
114.8: i.e., often repulsed by his wife Terentia, and then restored to grace.
114.9: e.g., in the Treaty of Brundisium (37 B.C.), and often during the Triumvirate.
114.12: i.e., from the second and first centuries B.C., back to the third century.
114.13: The latter a reasonable mark of good breeding, the former an ostentatious bit of effeminacy. Summers cites Ovid, A. A. i. 506 “don’t rub your legs smooth with the tight-scraping pumice stone.”
114.14: As Cicero (see Ep. xl. 11) was an example of the rhythmical in style, so Pollio is the representative of the “bumpy” (salebrosa) manner (Ep. c. 7).
114.16: For these Sallust fragments see the edition of Kritz, Nos. 33, Jug. 37. 4, and 42; for Arruntius see H. Peter, Frag. Hist. Rom. ii. pp. 41 f.
114.18: 18.0 18.1 18.2 “Brought to pass flight for our men”; “Hiero, king of the Syracusans brought about war”; “The news brought the men of Panormus” (now Palermo, Sicily) “to the point of surrendering to the Romans.”
114.19: 19.0 19.1 19.2 19.3 “Amid the wintry waters”; “The storm suddenly grew wintry”; “The whole year was like winter”; “Then he dispatched sixty transports of light draught besides the soldiers and the necessary sailors amid a wintry storm.”
114.20: The peculiarity here is the use of the plural instead of the singular form. “Amid civil war he seeks reminders of justice and virtue.”

115.1: Elsewhere (Epp. lxxvi. 2 and lxxxvii. 9) called trossuli, “fops.”
115.6: Cf. Nauck, Trag. Gr. fragg. adesp. 181. 1 and 461.
115.7: Cf. id., Eurip. Danaë, Frag. 324, and Hense’s note (ed. of 1914, p. 559).
115.8: A play on the compositio of rhetoric.

116.1: For a discussion of ἀπάθεια see Epp. ix. 2 ff. and lxxxv. 3 ff.

117.1: For this sort of discussion see Ep. cxiii. 1 ff.
117.2: This adjective expetibilis is found in Tacitus, Ann. xvi. 21, and in Boethius, Cons. ii. 6.
117.3: i.e., the Stoics as mentioned above (with whom Seneca often disagrees on minor details).
117.4: i.e., the external things; see Ep. xciii. 7 and note,—defined more specifically in § 9 below.
117.5: Presumably an allusion to the syllogistic enthusiasts rather than to Lucillius and his like.

118.1: i.e., solvendo aeri alieno, “in a position to pay one’s debts.”
118.3: Ad Att. i. 12. 1: “Even his relatives can’t screw a penny out of Caecilius at less than 12 per cent” (Winstedt).
118.4: For the character of Vatinius see Ep. xciv. 25 note; for a similar comparison of V. with Cato see Ep. cxx. 19.
118.5: Discussed in Ep. lxxi. 4 f., lxxiv. 30, lxxvi. 16 ff., and especially lxxxvii. 25: nam idem est honestum et bonum. The Academic school tended to draw more of a distinction than the Stoic, as in Ep. lxxxv. 17 f.
118.6: This argument (that complete virtue is a sort of transforming climax of life) is not to be confused with the theory of accessio (a term used also in Roman law), or “addition”; for virtue does not permit of accessio, or the addition of any external advantage. See Ep. lxvi. 9 quid accedere perfecto potest?

119.1: Seneca here reverts to the money-metaphors of Epp. i.–xxxiii.—lucellum, munusculum, diurna mercedula, etc.
119.3: i.e., “something for one’s spare time”; cf. Ep. liii. 8 note, non est quod precario philosopheris.
119.5: i.e., had got my coffers ready for the promised wealth.
119.7: i.e., a “poverty” which is never satisfied.

120.1: i.e., the Peripatetic and Academic schools.
120.3: Consult Sandys, Hist. Class. Schol. i. pp. 148 and 175 f. Alexandrian “analogists” opposed Pergamene “anomalists” with reference to the rules affecting the forms of words. Out of the controversy arose the scientific study of grammar.
120.4: The two stories refer to the years 280 and 279 B.C., during the campaigns of Pyrrhus in Italy.
120.6: Livy (loc cit.) reports him as saying: “Tiberine pater, te sancte precor, haec arma et hunc militem propitio flumine accipias!” Macaulay in his ballad translates Livy’s quotation almost literally.
120.7: A chronic disease of Seneca himself. See the autobiographic fragment in Ep. lxxviii. 1 f.
120.8: Seneca is here developing the thought sketched in Ep. xii. 6 unus autem dies gradus vitae est.
120.9: For the same contrast cf. Ep. cxviii. 4 (and note). For the following names see Index of Proper Names.

121.1: i.e., in addition to myself and confirming my statement.
121.4: i.e., their physical make-up, the elements of their physical being.
121.5: i.e., the “soul of the world,” of which each living soul is a part. The Stoics believed that it was situated in the heart. Zeno called it ἡγεμονικόν, “ruling power”; while the Romans used the term principale or principatus. The principle described above is ὀρμή (impulse) or τόνος (tension).
121.6: Seneca is both sound and modern in his account of animal “intelligence.” It is instinct, due to sensory-motor reactions, and depending largely upon type heredity.
121.7: A theme developed by Cicero (De fin. iii. 16): placet . . . simul atque natum sit animal . . . , ipsum sibi conciliari et commendari ad se conservandum.

122.4: In connexion with the Parentalia, Feb. 13–21, and at other anniversary observations, the ceremonies were held in the daytime.
122.5: A vice which Seneca especially abhors; cf. Ep. xv. 3 multum potionis altius ieiunio iturae.
122.6: By wearing silk gowns of transparent material.
122.7: Not literally translated. For the same thought see Ep. xlvii. 7, etc.
122.8: The symbols of a Roman funeral. For the same practice, purposely performed, see Ep. xii. 8 (and the note of W. C. Summers).
122.9: Called by Tacitus, Ann. iv. 34, a Seiani cliens.
122.11: i.e., Procne, in the well-known nightingale myth.
122.12: Son of the P. Vinicius ridiculed in Ep. xl. 9. He was husband of Julia, youngest daughter of Germanicus, and was poisoned by Messalina.
122.13: i.e., is punishing his slaves for errors in the day’s work.
122.14: i.e., balancing the custom of the ordinary Roman, whose dinner never continued beyond nightfall.
122.15: “’A liver by candle-light,’ with a play on the word λίχνος, ‘luxurious’” (Summers).

123.1: i.e., a dinner given by an official when he entered upon (adeo) his office.
123.2: For symphonia see Ep. li. 4 and note. Compare also the commissiones, orchestral exhibitions, composed of many voices, flutes, and brass instruments, Ep. lxxxiv. 10.
123.3: i.e., to live by Stoicism rather than by Epicureanism.
123.4: Meaning, in line with the Stoic paradoxes, that only the sage knows how to be rightly in love.

124.3: i.e., the advocate of the “touch” theory.
124.4: According to the Stoics (and other schools also), the “innate notions,” or groundwork of knowledge, begin to be subject to reason after the attainment of a child’s seventh year.
124.5: i.e., they are limited to “practical judgment.”
124.6: Just as Academic and Peripatetic philosophers sometimes defined as “goods” what the Stoics called “advantages.”
124.7: One of the most conspicuous Stoic paradoxes maintained that “the wise man is a God.”
